one_line_takeaway: By replacing random poisoning with TD-error-prioritized critical-sample targeting, in-distribution correlation-breaking triggers, and manifold-constrained gradient-guided adversarial actions, CS-GBA backdoors conservative offline-RL algorithms (CQL/IQL/BCQ) more effectively at half the poisoning budget (5% vs 10%) than the prior state-of-the-art BAFFLE, while avoiding the outright policy collapse that undermines BAFFLE's apparent success on unstable environments like Hopper.
key_finding: CS-GBA at a 5% poisoning budget outperforms BAFFLE at a 10% budget across nearly all settings. Representative numbers from the paper's own Table 1 (Walker2d-medium, Distributed-10 trigger): against CQL, CS-GBA drives Attack Reward to 452 vs BAFFLE's 1336 (Clean Reward 3430 vs 3402); against IQL, CS-GBA reaches 141 vs BAFFLE's 511 (near-random-policy level); against BCQ, CS-GBA reaches 372 vs BAFFLE's 1081. On HalfCheetah-medium (Table 2), CS-GBA achieves both higher stealth and higher attack potency simultaneously (e.g., IQL Distributed-10: CS-GBA Attack Reward 2395 vs BAFFLE 3367; Clean Reward 4672 vs 4641). On Hopper-medium (Table 3) the paper highlights a 'false positive' failure mode in BAFFLE: BAFFLE's CQL attack reward of 29 looks devastating but is accompanied by Clean Reward collapsing to 951 (from a ~2000 benign baseline), i.e., BAFFLE destroys the policy outright rather than installing a selective backdoor, whereas CS-GBA achieves a comparably lethal Attack Reward of 33 while preserving Clean Reward at 1941. Ablations (Table 4-6, CQL victim) each attribute a substantial share of the effect to the individual component: TD-selection vs random selection on Walker2d Distributed-10 drops Attack Reward from 2972 (random) to 452 (TD); on Hopper Distributed-10 from 1031 (random) to 33 (TD). Correlation-breaking trigger vs median trigger on Hopper: Clean Reward 1941 (ours) vs 978 (median), showing the median trigger causes clean-performance collapse via feature confusion. Gradient-guided action vs inverted-actor-loss on Hopper: Clean Reward 1941 (ours) vs 997 (inverted loss, also collapse). Sensitivity analysis (Tables 7-9) shows attack leverage decays sharply outside the top TD-error percentile (Top 5-10% window is markedly weaker than Top 0-5%) and that simply doubling the budget to 10% increases attack potency further but at a severe stealth cost (e.g., Hopper Clean Reward collapses to 937 at epsilon=10% vs 1941 at epsilon=5%), which the authors use to justify 5% as the chosen operating point on the stealth-efficacy Pareto frontier.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is CS-GBA, a black-box data-poisoning backdoor attack framework for Offline Reinforcement Learning built on three novel components introduced by the authors: (1) a TD-error-based Critical Sample Selection strategy that pre-trains a proxy Q-network on the clean dataset and ranks transitions by absolute TD-error (delta_TD = r + gamma*max_a' Q_proxy(s',a') - Q_proxy(s,a)), targeting the top-epsilon fraction (default top 5%) for poisoning instead of random or frequency-based selection; (2) a Correlation-Breaking Trigger that picks a state dimension k with high Pearson correlation to other features and sets it to its dataset-wide 95th-percentile value while leaving correlated peers unchanged, so the trigger is in-distribution marginally but breaks the joint feature correlation (stealthy against OOD filters); and (3) Gradient-Guided Malicious Action Generation, which uses projected gradient descent on the proxy Q-network's gradient (a_tilde <- Proj_A(a - eta * grad_a Q_proxy)) starting from the original in-distribution action to find a locally worst-case action that stays within the behavioral manifold, paired with a relabeled maximum/target reward (rtarget = 75th percentile of dataset rewards per the algorithm box) to force policy induction via the Bellman update.
threat_model: Black-box data-poisoning-only threat model against Offline RL. The attacker has full read/write access to the static offline dataset D_clean = {(s_i,a_i,r_i,s'_i)} and can modify state, action, and reward of a selected subset of transitions, but cannot alter the victim's training algorithm, loss function, hyperparameters, or environment dynamics. The attack is budget-constrained: |D_poison \ D_clean| <= epsilon|D_clean| with epsilon as low as 5%. Attacker goals are two conflicting objectives: (1) clean performance parity with a benign policy on untriggered states, and (2) upon trigger activation, force the victim policy to execute a specific low-value adversarial action a-dagger that degrades cumulative reward. Victim algorithms targeted are conservatism-based offline RL methods: CQL (Q-value penalization of OOD actions), IQL (expectile regression avoiding OOD queries), and BCQ (generative-model action-support constraint).
limitations: The paper's evaluation is confined to three MuJoCo continuous-control D4RL 'medium' datasets and only three offline-RL algorithms (CQL, IQL, BCQ); no results are given for other D4RL dataset qualities (e.g., medium-replay, expert) or for other architecture families (e.g., decision-transformer-style offline RL, model-based offline RL). It compares against a single baseline attack (BAFFLE) plus internally-substituted ablation variants (random selection, median trigger, inverted-actor-loss action) rather than against multiple independently published prior attacks (e.g., BadRL, which is cited as related work but not run as a head-to-head baseline in the tables). The attack relies on training a proxy Q-network on the clean dataset to compute TD-errors and action gradients -- the paper does not report how attack performance degrades if the attacker's proxy model architecture/training diverges from the victim's, nor does it evaluate any candidate defenses or detection methods against the correlation-breaking trigger itself (i.e., no adaptive-defense or detectability-metric evaluation is presented, only downstream clean/attack reward outcomes). It is labeled a 'Technical Report' rather than a peer-reviewed publication, and no code/artifact availability is mentioned in the paper.

quantitative_results: Walker2d/CQL/Distributed-10: CS-GBA Attack Reward 452 vs BAFFLE 1336 (Clean: 3430 vs 3402)
Walker2d/IQL/Distributed-10: CS-GBA Attack Reward 141 vs BAFFLE 511
Walker2d/BCQ/Distributed-10: CS-GBA Attack Reward 372 vs BAFFLE 1081
HalfCheetah/IQL/Distributed-10: CS-GBA Attack Reward 2395 vs BAFFLE 3367
Hopper/CQL/Distributed-10: CS-GBA Attack Reward 33 (Clean 1941) vs BAFFLE Attack Reward 29 but Clean Reward collapses to 951 (model collapse)
Hopper/BCQ/Distributed-10: CS-GBA 1139 vs BAFFLE 2043; Distributed-20: CS-GBA 1605 vs BAFFLE 2110
Ablation - sample selection, Walker2d Distributed-10 (CQL): TD-selection Attack Reward 452 vs Random-selection 2972
Ablation - sample selection, Hopper Distributed-10 (CQL): TD-selection 33 vs Random-selection 1031
Ablation - trigger design, Hopper Clean Reward: Correlation-breaking (ours) 1941 vs Median trigger 978
Ablation - action generation, Hopper Clean Reward: Gradient-guided (ours) 1941 vs Inverted-actor-loss 997
Sensitivity - Walker2d/CQL Distributed-10: Top 0-5% (eps=5%) Attack Reward 452 vs Top 5-10% (eps=5%) 1580 vs Top 0-10% (eps=10%) 213 (Clean Reward 3430 / 3087 / 2382 respectively)
Sensitivity - Hopper/CQL: Clean Reward collapses to 937 at eps=10% vs 1941 at eps=5% (Top 0-5%)
baselines_compared: BAFFLE (Gong et al. 2024, IEEE S&P) at 10% poisoning budget with random poisoning, median-based triggers, and inverted-actor-loss action generation
Random sample selection (ablation baseline for TD-error selection)
Median trigger (ablation baseline for correlation-breaking trigger)
Inverted actor loss action generation (ablation baseline for gradient-guided action generation)
